questions.question: Coulomb's Law for the force between two point charges is:
questions.explanation: Coulomb's Law: $F = k\frac{Q_1Q_2}{r^2}$. The force is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance.

questions.question: If the magnitude of one charge is doubled while the other charge and distance remain unchanged, the electrostatic force:
questions.explanation: $F \propto Q_1$. Doubling one charge doubles the force.

questions.question: If the distance between two charges is doubled, the electrostatic force becomes:
questions.explanation: $F \propto 1/r^2$. Doubling $r$ reduces the force to $1/2^2 = 1/4$ of the original.

questions.question: Three charges lie in a straight line. Charge A ($+4.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$) is $0.20\text{ m}$ left of Charge B ($-2.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$). Charge C ($+3.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$) is $0.10\text{ m}$ right of Charge B. What is the net electrostatic force on Charge B? ($k = 9.0 \times 10^9\text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$)
questions.explanation: Force from A on B: $F_{AB} = k\frac{(4.0 \times 10^{-6})(2.0 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.20)^2} = 1.8\text{ N}$ toward A (left). Force from C on B: $F_{CB} = k\frac{(3.0 \times 10^{-6})(2.0 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.10)^2} = 5.4\text{ N}$ toward C (right). Net $= 5.4 - 1.8 = 3.6\text{ N}$ to the right.

questions.question: Three charges lie in a straight line. Charge X ($+5.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$) is $0.30\text{ m}$ left of Charge Y ($+2.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$). Charge Z ($-4.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$) is $0.15\text{ m}$ right of Charge Y. What is the net electrostatic force on Charge Y? ($k = 9.0 \times 10^9\text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$)
questions.explanation: Force from X on Y (both +, repulsive): $F_{XY} = k\frac{(5.0 \times 10^{-6})(2.0 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.30)^2} = 1.0\text{ N}$ to the right. Force from Z on Y (opposite, attractive): $F_{ZY} = k\frac{(4.0 \times 10^{-6})(2.0 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.15)^2} = 3.2\text{ N}$ to the right. Both act rightward. Net $= 1.0 + 3.2 = 4.2\text{ N}$ to the right.

questions.question: Two charges of $2.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$ and $3.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$ are placed $0.10\text{ m}$ apart. What is the force between them? ($k = 9.0 \times 10^9\text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$)
What causes a neutral atom to acquire a net positive charge?
options: A net gain of an electron
A net loss of an electron
A net gain of a proton
A net loss of a proton
questions.explanation: A net positive charge means the atom has fewer electrons than protons. This occurs when an electron is transferred away, leaving behind an excess positive charge from the protons in the nucleus, which remain fixed and cannot be gained or lost through standard electrical interactions.

questions.question: Three charges lie in a straight line. Charge P ($-3.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$) is $0.20\text{ m}$ left of Charge Q ($+6.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$). Charge R ($-2.0 \times 10^{-6}\text{ C}$) is $0.40\text{ m}$ right of Charge Q. What is the net electrostatic force on Charge Q? ($k = 9.0 \times 10^9\text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$)
questions.explanation: Force from P on Q (attractive): $F_{PQ} = k\frac{(3.0 \times 10^{-6})(6.0 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.20)^2} = 4.05\text{ N}$ toward P (left). Force from R on Q (attractive): $F_{RQ} = k\frac{(2.0 \times 10^{-6})(6.0 \times 10^{-6})}{(0.40)^2} = 0.675\text{ N}$ toward R (right). Net $= 4.05 - 0.675 \approx 3.4\text{ N}$ to the left.

questions.question: Two charges $Q$ and $q$ are separated by distance $r$. If both charges are doubled and the distance is also doubled, the new force is:
options: The same as the original force
questions.explanation: $F' = k\frac{(2Q)(2q)}{(2r)^2} = k\frac{4Qq}{4r^2} = k\frac{Qq}{r^2} = F$. The changes cancel.

questions.question: The value of the Coulomb constant $k$ is approximately:
questions.explanation: $k = 9.0 \times 10^9\text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$. Option D is the gravitational constant $G$, a common confusion.

questions.question: Two electrically neutral objects are placed near each other. The electrostatic force between them is:
questions.explanation: Neutral objects have no net charge, so there is no Coulomb force between them.

questions.question: Both Coulomb's Law and Newton's Law of Gravitation are:
questions.explanation: Both forces follow $F \propto 1/r^2$ — they are inverse square laws. This is a deep parallel between electrostatics and gravity.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered Coulomb's Law and electrostatic force!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the inverse square relationship and the units of $k$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on $F = kQ_1Q_2/r^2$ and how force changes with charge and distance.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: Coulomb's Law formula, like charges repel, unlike charges attract, and the value of $k$.
